mnemonics.title: G.I.F.T for Grid Computing
mnemonics.content: G.I.F.T stands for: Geographical distribution, Independent resources, Federated architecture, Throughput-oriented - key characteristics of grid computing that differentiates it from traditional HPC

mnemonics.title: C.L.U.S.T.E.R for Cluster Computing
mnemonics.content: C.L.U.S.T.E.R stands for: Coupled systems, Load balancing, Unified resource, Single system image, Tightly coupled, Ethernet/network connected, Redundancy - core components of cluster computing architecture

mnemonics.title: D.I.S.T.R.I.B.U.T.E.D
mnemonics.content: D.I.S.T.R.I.B.U.T.E.D stands for: Decentralized control, Independent nodes, Scalability, Transparency, Reliability, Independence, Balance load, Unified coordination, Task distribution, Error handling, Deadlock prevention - principles of distributed computing

mnemonics.title: M.P.I. P.I.N.G
mnemonics.content: MPI PING: Point-to-Point Communication, Interactive, Non-blocking, Group communication - remember the two primary communication paradigms in parallel computing

acronyms.usage: Standardized communication protocol used in cluster computing for parallel processes; use when writing parallel programs across multiple nodes
API for shared-memory parallelism in cluster computing; use when parallelizing single-node multi-threaded applications
Used to describe computing systems that aggregate computing power to solve complex problems; applies to both grid and cluster computing
Software that manages network resources in distributed systems; understand its role in cluster management
Emulated computing environment; fundamental to cloud computing and enables resource virtualization in grid/cluster setups
Cloud service models; IaaS is closest to grid/cluster computing (infrastructure level), PaaS provides platform, SaaS provides software
LAN used in cluster computing (tightly coupled), WAN used in grid computing (loosely coupled across地理区域)

visualTips.visualization: Picture a national power grid - electricity comes from multiple power plants (resources) across the country, is distributed through a network, and users access it without knowing where it originated. Similarly, grid computing aggregates distributed resources

Imagine a row of identical servers in a data center, like a stack of textbooks on a desk - they work together as one unit, share the same bookshelf (network), and are managed by one system (single system image)
Visualize a classroom - the teacher (master node) distributes assignments (tasks) to students (slave/compute nodes), collects completed work, and manages the entire process
Think of a restaurant with multiple waiters - customers (requests) are distributed evenly among available waiters (nodes) to prevent any one waiter from being overwhelmed
Picture a backup goalkeeper in soccer - if the main node fails, backup nodes automatically take over, ensuring the game (computation) continues without interruption
Imagine reading a book from your own shelf vs. traveling to a library - bringing computation to where data resides (data locality) is faster than moving large data across the network

analogies.analogy: Like a cooperative of farmers - each farmer (organization) has their own land and equipment, they share surplus resources during harvest season (computational tasks), and coordinate through a central marketplace (grid middleware) without losing ownership of their assets
Like a team of rowing crew - all oarsmen (compute nodes) must be synchronized, face the same direction, and work as a single unit under one coxswain (master node) to move the boat efficiently

Distributed computing is like multiple independent construction teams building different houses in different neighborhoods (loose coupling), while parallel computing is like multiple workers building the same house together (tight coupling)
Like a budget supercomputer built from off-the-shelf gaming PCs connected together - anyone can build it using commodity hardware, similar to how Beowulf clusters use standard PCs with Linux
Thin client is like a simple terminal that only displays (like a monitor with minimal processing), while thick client is like a full-featured desktop that does all the processing locally

Like a hotel (physical server) with multiple rooms (virtual machines) - each room has its own space, privacy, and can be decorated differently (different OS/configurations), but they all share the same building infrastructure
